So, you know, the pitch really starts with the fact that the traditional SOC model is broken where there's a heavy reliance on humans.
Throughout the entire process from end to end.
And, you know, I think the biggest bottleneck that tends to happen.
is around alerting.
So the fact is there's just not enough humans to monitor all the different signals that need to be monitored to keep organizations secure in, you know, 2026.
And a lot of this stems from the fact that data volumes have continually increased year over year for, you know, 15, 20 years.
It's never going to stop.
And AI is actually just going to make it worse because there's going to be a massive proliferation of new applications that come up.
And just a massive growth of existing ones.
So the, you know, the median log volume that enterprises see is around four terabytes of logs per day, which is growing each year.
And four terabytes of logs per day was previously seen as like an impossible volume, you know, maybe 10 years ago for any sim.
And now that's just the standard, you know, to really just go from zero to one in a lot of these big enterprises.
But the, the problems sort of like flow downstream.
So you have a massive influx of data.

And signals that have to be monitored from that data, you have a massive amount of alerts as well that are related to certain behaviors that could indicate suspicious or, like, high-risk behavior that could lead to, you know, damage, it could lead to a potential breach, or, you know, something that could cause harm to the organization.
And the fact of the matter is that we don't have enough eyes and time to look at all this signal, so what ends up happening is, you know, 60% of them get ignored.
And, you know, there's a lot of devil in the details in cyber, and there's a lot of high nuance as well.
So it just, the amount of time, it just doesn't work, like, the math doesn't work, ultimately.
And, you know, this flows into the fact that breaches, you know, are continuously getting expensive, and they get more expensive the longer the dwell time.
So as breaches continue over time, you know, the organization ends up paying disproportionate amounts of money and also reputation.
So there's a lot of challenges that exist in the modern SOC model.
And, you know, our perspective is similar to the rest of industry of where can we apply AI in strategic ways to alleviate these problems?
So the way that Panther really approaches this problem is, you know, our core strength is in detection response across petabytes of log data.
So we want to be the most scalable SIEM, and we want to alleviate all these issues with good technology choices, with AI agents, with code-based workflows, and highly structured data.
We think that these decisions strategically position us as a product to alleviate a lot of these problems and flow these benefits downstream into security teams.
So what we end up seeing, you know, with our platform, which we're, you know, we will be calling, you know, a complete AI SOC platform, where we apply AI across the entire lifecycle.
Detection and response to alleviate these core problems.
What we end up seeing is when you apply AI and techniques like streaming analysis, which is our predominant way of analyzing data, we see a massive, massive reduction of triage time.
And this really helps compress that time from an event happening in your organization or in your production environments to actually having a decision on what to do next.
So, you know, previously it would take maybe, you know, 15 to 30 minutes to even get the alert.
And then it would take another 15 to 30 minutes to triage the alert.
And now we can compress that down into a few minutes.
So that's going to continue getting better over time.
But the latency has always been something that we want to continually drive down.
And that's made possible through AI agents and streaming analysis, which is our primary way of doing detection.
The further, you know, downstream benefit as well is that we can actually take more data in because.
Because of these decisions that we've made around our architecture.
So Panther is extremely, extremely scalable.
can handle, you know, upwards of 100 terabytes of log data per day.
And, you know, on the high end, our customers are doing multiple petabytes of logs per month.
And that benefit flows into alerting as well, where it means the security team can continually onboard more signals.
And they can scale their volume without having to scale their headcount linearly.
And a lot of that benefit also comes from the fact that we are heavily code driven.
So the predominant nature of doing detection in Panther is using Python.
But now with AI agents, you don't have to write the Python directly.
just express what you want in natural language.
It will write the detection.
will tune it for you, et cetera.
And I'll talk about how AI plays a role across this whole life cycle.
But there's a ton of benefits with these decisions that we've made, allowing teams to have much higher throughput and scale.
For, you know, the next five, 10 years.
And then finally cost,
Everything has to make sense economically.
There's always a trade-off between where we invest in compute and agents and all these things, and compute cost is going to be a huge bottleneck going into the next 10 years with agents.
So it always has to be top of mind.

We always need to be building sustainable programs, but the decisions that we've made allow security teams, both in our technology and pricing model choices, to save at least 50% on what it costs them prior with legacy sim.
And we just think that these sort of primitives around AI agents and serverless and these decisions that we've made have allowed our customers to have an unfair advantage against attackers.
So that's kind of like the high level of kind of what we do and what the benefits end up being.
And then before I just go into like the downstream of like the how, is there any context I can add on like the problem statement and the values?
No, like this, this is helpful, I guess, maybe just a little bit more on the personal side, like what inspired you to want to come and solve this problem?
Like, give me a little bit of the background, like leading up to Panther.

Yeah, so before Panther, was a security analyst, security engineer, working in Silicon Valley tech companies for about eight years, maybe round up to 10, if I count like some work I did in college, but worked in cyber teams for, you know, the first half of my career.
And then during the time at Yahoo, which is where, you know, I went to work out of college, we just kept finding that the tools available for us at our scale never worked.
And it required us to build a lot of scaffolding around the tools.
And when we went to, when I joined Airbnb, it was really the same type of thing where we didn't want to just roll a traditional.
Because we knew it would be too expensive, too slow, too limited in terms of its detection capability and automation capabilities.
So we decided to build our own, which isn't always the best decision, obviously.
But it did work out really well for us because we open sourced the project and it became widely adopted in the community.
And those primitives really were the precursor into what Panther is now.
And obviously, Panther is like the enterprise version.
And it's used by even more companies, very similar to the ones that had adopted StreamAlert, which was the open source project.
So that's kind of like the brief history of it.
It was just more of like feeling that problem as a practitioner and then wanting to really think from first principles how to address the problem.
And then now with AI, it's like the evolution of that.
We're kind of in our like second wave of our product.
So it's pretty exciting.
Yeah, that's amazing.
Such an exciting time.
Yeah.

Is leading the pack across our competitors.

But we've done an exceptional job of pursuing AI and listening to our customers and just making fast decisions.

So we talked about the what we do, the benefits, and this is really the how.
So Panther spans four key areas of detection and response today, and each one of them is complemented by AI in a slightly different way.
But the whole idea is that we want to apply AI agents across this entire lifecycle to make the entire system smarter.
And we're seeing really great success so far in our AI SOC analyst, which was the first agent that we had introduced.
So all data in Panther is highly structured, and that's by design to enable the detectionist code, to enable the data lake, and now it's actually a massive benefit with AI agents.
So we highly structure all the data coming in.
Teams can filter, they can drop, they can do a lot of various ETL and...
You know, data filtering type of work there.
It's quite robust.
think it's actually pretty underrated in terms of our platform.
But once all the data gets structured, it gets put into a data lake.
I'm showing the data lake here as Databricks because that was a presentation that I just gave.
But it can actually be Databricks or Snowflake.
So I need to have an updated version of the slide that sort of shows either or.
And the next step of this process is detection.
So we have a pretty robust detection correlation engine.
It allows teams to declare various types of attacker techniques and tactics.
So, for example, we can do more trivial detection that's looking at just certain patterns and logs.
That's very low latency.
We can do batch-based analysis, which looks backwards in time and does interesting aggregates and anomaly detection.
And then we can do correlations as well where we're looking at sequences and batches of activity.
So there's a lot of different ways to model the behaviors that are very important for an organization.
And then on the right side of that is alerting.
So this is once the detection engine has detected something that's relevant and important and urgent, it will create an alert.
will page your team through whatever means you typically use.
And then we actually now use AI in the middle of that.

So what happens is we're building towards a path where we have an autonomous AI SOC analyst that will look at every single alert and will only truly escalate the things that are risky or inconclusive for your security team to take a look at.
And that removes a massive amount of just sort of like baseline noise that is always happening.
And that might be things like repetitive alerts.
It could be something that was an engineer making a benign change.
But the fact is that we can actually increase our coverage to 100%.
So, you know, we're not ignoring half of the alerts anymore.
And then it gives the security team a lot of breathing room to focus on the whole system and making sure.
Making that we're getting the right data, that we're giving the AI agents the right context, and we're creating more detections and more signal for the AI to continue to analyze.
So that's a really exciting part of the platform that we have today.
I forgot to mention, on detection, we're introducing a detection engineer, detection writer agent very soon, and that will allow security teams to interact with the detections in natural language.

So they can say, like, hey, I want to create a rule for anytime someone's logging into Okta from, you know, not a US or Europe country, and, you know, they have, like, five or more failed logins or something, right?
You can express that just with natural language, it will create the detection, and then you can refine it as well, once you see certain alerts.
But we want to make this loop more automated over time, and that's what we're planning to do.
The final piece is really around the data at rest, so because everything is highly structured.

It is very easily queryable, and we really think that where it's going is, you know, like a ChatGPT style of exploration of the data, where you're not doing it directly, you're just expressing what you're looking for, and the agent will go write all the queries for you, it will synthesize all the results, and then you can sort of steer the agent where to go next.
But we do give direct access into the data through a pipe-based query language.
So, yeah, this is in a nutshell, you know, how we express our AI SOC platform.
We give security teams these four core parts of the system.
We augment each of them with AI, and we connect them all together with AI as well.
So, what the AI SOC analyst does on the alerting layer is transferable knowledge into the detection layer, which gets transferred into the co-pilot.
Like, they're all able to access this, like, shared sort of synthesized learning.
And that's really what makes
It the product really strong and it's what customers are gravitating towards.

All data...

And then customers can see the data, the fields, the description of the fields.
And then we also have a field discovery feature where if the schema changes in drifts over time, Panther will automatically adapt to that schema, and then you will be able to search all of that data.
So we've done a lot in this area.
And we've also done a lot on schema inference as well.
So you can upload sample logs.
It will generate those schemas for you using AI, and then you can save and apply it to your sections.
let me just pull a random one.

can filter by the various log types.

this is solving for the gap, I think, Katie, you mentioned on the call Monday where you don't need to use Python anymore.

Yeah, well, think it depends a little on the role of that person.

So a security engineer is more likely to be on the detection layer and maybe like the automation layer, whereas analysts will be more so on like the alert queue and using agents to really like triage, which I'm about to show.
So maybe I can progress into that.
But yeah, the detection, you know, AI agent for detection, whatever we end up calling it, is super helpful for creating those detections.
And then we get alerts about those detections.
So we use AI to autonomously respond to these alerts.
And what you end up getting is a really nice summary of really everything that happened related to this behavior and things that are highly related to that.
So it's very uncommon that an attacker just does one thing.
When you get an alert, it's kind of...
A canary in the coal mine, so to say.
So we want to use our agent to do all that context building and synthesis and reporting.
So then what the analyst gets is a distilled summary, a judgment, and then they can make the next call based on their own intuition and context of what they know about the company.
So this alert specifically is me going through and changing an alert destination severity routing.
So that basically just means I updated a alert destination to where it won't get alerts anymore.
And this could be indicative of someone as a malicious insider.
It could be someone who gained access to the sim and wants to effectively disable defenses.
So we have agents that are running on all of these alerts.
And the way that it effectively works is it will use all the data and tools in Panther as disposal.
So it will do things like getting enrichments on IPs.
It will do

Things like reading the detection logic, will look at alerts around the time of this detection, and then it will do things like writing pivot queries to look at Jack's, you know, administrative actions around the alert time.
So this is looking at, let's see, what was the time?
It was at 20.
So this is about a 30-minute buffer around, and then we're annotating exactly why it's pivoting, which is really, really helpful.
So this one is looking at a related behavior that was detected around that same time because we are looking at the other alerts.
So this found two related alerts.
And then what we ended up getting is this, like, synthesis of what it found.
So it gives me who I am.
So I'm the CTO founder.
I did a certain behavior, and then that is dictated as risky.
And it's dictated as risky because we have certain evidence that was collected.
So it's looking at things like the enrichments, it's looking at my behavior around that time, and then it's calculating a score that goes into that final judgment.
And then we try to elevate the most important aspects of this behavior to our security analysts that are using our platform.
And we keep it as like a three to five bullet summary.
We explain, you know, what happened around the same time.
We give a really nice visual flowchart of that behavior as well.
Which is really helpful.
think humans are very visual, obviously.
